title: Working with Data
tagline: Dictionaries, files, error handling, JSON, and string processing.

lessons.title: Store Data with Dictionaries

accomplishments: Store related data together (name + age + email in one place)
Access data by name instead of numbers
Build real user profiles and contact lists
Understand why dictionaries power every database

do.title: Create your first dictionary
steps: Run it — see your user profile data!
do.code:
user = {
    'name': 'Jenna',
    'age': 28,
    'city': 'Chicago',
    'is_developer': True
}

print(user)
print("Name:", user['name'])
print("City:", user['city'])

# Add a new key
user['email'] = 'jenna@example.com'
print("Updated:", user)
do.result: You stored multiple pieces of related data in ONE variable, accessed by meaningful names instead of index numbers!
do.proofRequired: your dictionary with at least 5 key-value pairs

concepts.name: Dictionaries
concepts.definition: A collection that stores key-value pairs. Access data by name (key) instead of position (index). Keys must be unique.
concepts.analogy: A phone book — you look up a name (key) to find a number (value). You don't search by position.

concepts.name: Key-Value Pairs
concepts.definition: Each item is a pair: 'name': 'Jenna'. The key is the label, the value is the data. Keys must be unique strings or numbers.
concepts.analogy: Labeled drawers in a filing cabinet. The label (key) tells you what's inside (value).

concepts.name: Dictionary Methods
concepts.definition: .keys() returns all keys, .values() returns all values, .items() returns key-value pairs, .get() safely accesses with a default, .pop() removes a key.
concepts.analogy: Tools for managing your filing cabinet — list all labels, find specific drawers, remove old files.

concepts.name: Nested Dictionaries
concepts.definition: Dictionaries inside dictionaries for complex real-world data. Like a user profile with an address sub-object.
concepts.analogy: A folder inside a folder — organized layers of information.

understand.keyTakeaway: Dictionaries store data by name, not position. They're the foundation of real-world data structures — user profiles, configs, API responses.

build.goal: Build a user profile system with nested data
build.hint: Access nested data by chaining brackets: user['address']['city']

challenge.title: Build a Contact Management System
challenge.mission: Create a contact book with add, search, and update functionality.
requirements: Create a list of 3+ contact dictionaries
Each contact has: name, phone, email, city
Function to find contacts by city
challenge.bonusChallenge: Save contacts to a JSON file (preview of the JSON lesson!).

bridge.preview: Next: File Handling — read from and write to actual files on your computer.

lessons.devFession: I stored user data as a list: ['Jenna', 28, 'Chicago']. Then I forgot which index was what. Was [1] the age or the city? Dictionaries solved that forever.

lessons.title: Read and Write Files

accomplishments: Read data from actual files on your computer
Write data that persists after the program closes
Build a note-taking app that saves to disk
Understand file paths and file modes

do.title: Write your first file
steps: Run it — check your folder for 'my_note.txt'!
do.code:
# Write to a file
with open('my_note.txt', 'w') as file:
    file.write('Hello from Python!\n')
    file.write('This is my first file.\n')
    file.write('I can save data permanently now!')

print('File created!')

# Read it back
with open('my_note.txt', 'r') as file:
    content = file.read()
    print(content)
do.result: You just wrote data to a real file on your computer — it persists even after the program ends!
do.proofRequired: your code AND the my_note.txt file open in another window

concepts.name: File Handling
concepts.definition: Reading from and writing to files on your hard drive. Data persists — it's there when you come back.
concepts.analogy: Saving a Word document. The data stays on your computer until you delete it.

concepts.name: File Modes
concepts.definition: 'r' = read, 'w' = write (overwrites!), 'a' = append (adds to end), 'r+' = read and write.
concepts.analogy: Different tools for different jobs: reading glasses (r), a fresh notebook (w), a pen to add notes (a).

concepts.name: The 'with' Statement
concepts.definition: Safe way to work with files. Automatically closes the file when done, even if an error occurs.
concepts.analogy: A self-closing door. You walk in, do your work, and it closes behind you automatically.

concepts.name: Reading Methods
concepts.definition: .read() gets entire file, .readline() gets one line, .readlines() gets all lines as a list, for loop processes line by line (best for large files).
concepts.analogy: Different ways to read a book: the whole thing, one page at a time, or the table of contents.

understand.keyTakeaway: Files let your programs save data permanently. Always use 'with' to handle files safely.

codeComparison.new:
from datetime import datetime

def add_entry(text):
    timestamp = datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')
    with open('journal.txt', 'a') as f:
        f.write(f'[{timestamp}] {text}\n')

def read_journal():
    with open('journal.txt', 'r') as f:
        print(f.read())

add_entry('Started learning file handling!')
read_journal()

build.hint: Use 'a' mode to append without deleting existing entries.

challenge.title: Build a Personal Journal App
challenge.mission: Create a journal that saves entries with timestamps and can search by keyword.
requirements: Function to add entry with timestamp
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add the ability to delete entries by line number.

bridge.preview: Next: Error Handling — make your code bulletproof by handling problems instead of crashing.

lessons.devFession: I once used 'w' mode thinking it would append. It overwrote my entire file. 200 lines of data, gone. Now I triple-check my file mode every time.

lessons.title: Handle Errors Gracefully

accomplishments: Stop your programs from crashing
Handle errors gracefully instead of breaking
Validate user input before processing
Build bulletproof applications

do.title: Catch your first error
do.code:
# Without try-except: CRASH!
# number = int('hello')  # ValueError!

# With try-except: handled!
try:
    number = int('hello')
    print('Converted!')
except ValueError:
    print('That is not a valid number!')

print('Program continues running!')
do.result: Instead of crashing, your code caught the error and kept going. That's professional error handling!
do.proofRequired: your code handling at least 2 different error types

concepts.name: Exceptions
concepts.definition: Errors that occur while your program runs. Without handling, they crash the program. With try-except, you catch them gracefully.
concepts.analogy: A safety net under a tightrope. If you fall (error), the net (except) catches you.

concepts.name: Try-Except Blocks
concepts.definition: try: run this code. except: if it fails, do this instead. The program keeps running either way.
concepts.analogy: A plan B. Try plan A, but if it fails, switch to plan B instead of giving up.

concepts.name: Common Exception Types
concepts.definition: ValueError (wrong type), ZeroDivisionError (divide by 0), FileNotFoundError (missing file), KeyError (missing dict key), IndexError (list out of range).
concepts.analogy: Different types of mistakes need different fixes — like different medical specialties for different injuries.

concepts.name: Else and Finally
concepts.definition: else runs if NO error occurred. finally ALWAYS runs, even if there was an error. Great for cleanup.
concepts.analogy: Else = celebration after success. Finally = cleanup whether you won or lost.

understand.keyTakeaway: Professional code doesn't crash — it handles errors gracefully and keeps running.

build.goal: Build a safe number input function

codeComparison.old:
number = int(input('Enter number: '))
# Crashes if user types 'hello'!

build.hint: Use a while True loop with try-except to keep asking until valid input.

challenge.title: Build a Bulletproof Calculator
challenge.mission: Create a calculator that handles every possible error.
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a history feature that saves all calculations to a file.

bridge.preview: Next: JSON — the data format used by every API on the internet.

lessons.devFession: I deployed code to production without error handling. The first user typed a letter into the age field and the entire app crashed. For everyone. At the same time.

lessons.title: Work with JSON Data

accomplishments: Understand the data format used by every API
Convert Python dictionaries to JSON and back
Read and write JSON files
Parse real API responses

do.title: Convert between Python and JSON
do.code:
import json

user = {
    'name': 'Jenna',
    'age': 28,
    'skills': ['Python', 'JavaScript'],
    'is_developer': True
}

# Python → JSON string
json_string = json.dumps(user, indent=2)
print('JSON:', json_string)

# JSON string → Python dict
parsed = json.loads(json_string)
print('Name:', parsed['name'])

# Save to file
with open('user.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(user, f, indent=2)

print('Saved to user.json!')
do.result: You just converted Python data to JSON and back. This is how every app stores and exchanges data!
do.proofRequired: your code AND the user.json file it created

concepts.name: JSON
concepts.definition: JavaScript Object Notation — a text format for storing and exchanging data. Looks like Python dictionaries. Used by every API, database, and config file.
concepts.analogy: A universal language that all programming languages can read. Like English being the common language of business.

concepts.name: json.dumps() vs json.dump()
concepts.definition: dumps() converts to a JSON string (s = string). dump() writes directly to a file. loads() parses a string, load() reads from a file.
concepts.analogy: dumps = put it in a letter. dump = put it in the mailbox. loads = read the letter. load = check the mailbox.

concepts.name: JSON vs Python differences
concepts.definition: JSON uses true/false (lowercase), null (not None), double quotes only, no trailing commas. Python uses True/False, None, single or double quotes.
concepts.analogy: British vs American English — mostly the same, but some spelling differences.

understand.keyTakeaway: JSON is how the internet exchanges data. Master it and you can work with any API or data source.

build.goal: Build a settings system that saves to JSON
build.hint: Combine try-except with json.load() to handle missing config files gracefully.

challenge.title: Build a Contact Book with JSON Storage
challenge.mission: Create a contact book that persists data in a JSON file.
requirements: Store contacts in a JSON file
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add import/export functionality — export contacts to CSV format.

capabilities: Convert Python data to JSON and back

bridge.preview: Next: String Manipulation — clean, format, search, and transform text data.

lessons.devFession: I spent hours manually parsing API responses character by character before someone told me about json.loads(). One line replaced 50 lines of string splitting.

lessons.title: Master String Manipulation

accomplishments: Clean and format messy text data
Search and replace text patterns
Split and join strings
Validate emails and phone numbers
Build a text processing toolkit

do.title: Clean messy user input
do.code:
# Real-world data is MESSY
user_email = '  JENNA@EXAMPLE.COM  '
clean_email = user_email.strip().lower()

user_phone = '(555) 123-4567'
clean_phone = user_phone.replace('(', '').replace(')', '').replace(' ', '').replace('-', '')

user_name = '  jenna   martinez  '
clean_name = ' '.join(user_name.split()).title()

print(f"Email: '{clean_email}'")
print(f"Phone: '{clean_phone}'")
print(f"Name: '{clean_name}'")
do.result: You just cleaned real-world messy data into usable format. This is what professional developers do daily!
do.proofRequired: your code cleaning at least 3 different types of messy input

concepts.name: Essential String Methods
concepts.definition: .upper()/.lower() for case, .strip() removes whitespace, .replace() swaps text, .split() breaks into list, .join() combines list into string, .startswith()/.endswith() checks boundaries.
concepts.analogy: A Swiss Army knife for text — each tool handles a specific text transformation.

concepts.name: F-Strings
concepts.definition: Python 3.6+ formatted strings: f'Hello {name}, you are {age} years old'. Clean, readable, and the modern way to format.
concepts.analogy: Mad Libs — you write the template with blanks, Python fills them in.

concepts.name: String Checking Methods
concepts.definition: .isdigit() checks for numbers only, .isalpha() for letters only, .isalnum() for letters+numbers, .isspace() for whitespace.
concepts.analogy: ID checkers — each one verifies a different type of character.

understand.keyTakeaway: String manipulation is used in every app — cleaning input, formatting output, validating data. Master these methods.

build.hint: Use list comprehension with isdigit() to extract only numbers from a phone string.

challenge.title: Build a Text Processing Toolkit
challenge.mission: Create a library of text processing functions.
requirements: Function to clean names (trim, title case)
Function to clean emails (trim, lowercase, validate)
Function to generate username from name
Function to mask credit card (show last 4)
Function to create URL slug from title
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a function that counts word frequency in a text and returns the top 5 most common words.

bridge.preview: Module 2 complete! Next: Module 3 — APIs, web scraping, automation, and a capstone project.

lessons.devFession: I once displayed a user's email as 'JENNA@EXAMPLE.COM' in the UI because I forgot to .lower() it. The user emailed support asking why we were 'yelling' their email address.
